query: recent studies on KCNQ1 splicing modulation for arrhythmia treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Antiarrhythmic Medications -- Mechanism of Action -- Class VII Antiarrhythmic (upstream target modulators)
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac disease alters the function of ion channels, promoting cardiac rhythm disruptions, ie, "arrhythmogenic remodeling." Arrhythmogenic remodeling has important pathophysiological implications that majorly affect cardiac morbidity and mortality. Upstream therapy with ARBs, ACE inhibitors, statins, and omega-3 fatty acids that target remodeling and arrhythmogenesis can be efficacious but need further clinical evaluation. [17] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Jervell and Lange-Nielsen Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The first-line therapy in preventing syncope, cardiac arrest, and sudden death is a beta-blockade. Propranolol and nadolol have been shown to be superior to metoprolol in preventing cardiac events. The current consensus is that nadolol as the most preferred. [5] In patients with a history of cardiac arrest, an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) should be placed. Other indications for ICD placement is in patients who are deemed high risk. These include a QTc interval greater than 550 msec, syncope before age 5, and a male older than 20 years with the KCNQ1 pathogenic variant. [6] Despite beta-blocker therapy in symptomatic pediatric patients, Fruh et al. from Norway endorse the combined modality of atrial pacemaker and beta-blocker. [7] Hearing loss is usually treated successfully with cochlear implantation. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Keshan Disease -- Pathophysiology -- Genetic Mutations Involved in Keshan Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Another study investigated the human SCN5A gene, which encodes a sodium channel within the cardiomyocyte membrane. This study revealed a significant association between the H558R polymorphism in exon 12 of SCN5A and Keshan disease. This genetic variation was significantly associated with increased susceptibility to Keshan disease and demonstrated potential connections with QRS prolongation observed in electrocardiogram (ECG) measurements. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrophysiologic Study Indications and Evaluation -- Indications -- Inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes (Channelopathies)
retrieved_docs.content: Short QT syndrome (SQTS): These are congenital channelopathies with QTc <330ms associated with an increased risk of malignant ventricular arrhythmias leading to cardiac arrest. Early identification of high-risk patients and ICD implantation is important for secondary prevention of sudden cardiac death in these patients. EPS has low sensitivity (37%) for the detection of high-risk patients, but the negative predictive value is still modest at 58%. [24] Based on these studies, per the 2015 ESC guidelines use of EP study have Class III, LOE C in the evaluation of SQTS.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrophysiologic Study Indications and Evaluation -- Indications -- Inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes (Channelopathies)
retrieved_docs.content: Class I indication None Class II indication

retrieved_docs.title: Reentry Arrhythmia(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The Sicilian Gambit proposes a rational approach to antiarrhythmic drug selection. In contrast to empiric drug choice, this approach tries to identify the underlying mechanism of arrhythmogenesis to target drug therapy. The common form AVNRT uses slow pathway in antegrade conduction and retrograde fast pathway excitation. First-line therapy is the vagal maneuver. Drugs of choice include adenosine, beta blockers such as esmolol, and calcium channel blockers such as verapamil or diltiazem. All these drugs affect the slow conduction pathway. To target the fast pathway sodium channel blockers and amiodarone can be used. In orthodromic WPW syndrome, the excitation travels the AV node antegrade and the accessory pathway Kent bundle retrogradely. The length of the excitable gap can be evaluated in electrophysiologic studies. If the reentry is associated with a short excitable gap, potassium channel blocking agents such as amiodarone can be used to lengthen the refractory period of the accessory pathway. If the reentry is associated with a long excitable gap a sodium channel blocker may be chosen. [75]

retrieved_docs.title: Antiarrhythmic Medications -- Mechanism of Action -- Class VII Antiarrhythmic (upstream target modulators)
retrieved_docs.content: ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs), statins, and omega-3 fatty acids. These agents focus on tissue structure remodeling processes and, consequently, longer-term changes that contrast with the primary focus on the short-term effects of drugs on ion channels. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Junctional Ectopic Tachycardia -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Families should be counseled about the side effects and potential toxicities of antiarrhythmic medications like amiodarone, flecainide, and ivabradine, and the necessity of regular monitoring through ECGs and possibly thyroid, liver, and pulmonary function tests. For patients with postoperative JET, discussion about surgical risks, the importance of postoperative monitoring in the intensive care unit, and the generally transient nature of POJET is reassuring to caregivers. Additionally, education should include the avoidance of unnecessary stimuli or medications that may exacerbate arrhythmias postoperatively. JET can have genetic associations, particularly in CJET cases linked to mutations like TNNI3K , so families may also benefit from genetic counseling. Emphasis on multidisciplinary care, encompassing cardiology, electrophysiology, genetics, and primary care, is crucial for ensuring optimal long-term outcomes and minimizing the risk of complications or sudden cardiac events.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sodium Channels -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Dysfunction in voltage-gated sodium channels correlates with neurological and cardiac diseases, including epilepsy, myopathies, and cardiac arrhythmias (long QT, Brugada, sick sinus, and short QT syndromes, among others). The dysfunction can be genetic or acquired, including secondary to toxins. In particular, toxins from marine animals have a preference for blocking voltage-gated sodium channels, resulting in sensory abnormalities (because of dysfunction of sensory neurons) and muscle weakness (from the pathology of the motor neurons and muscle cells). One such toxin is the tetrodotoxin from the Tetraodontidae family, more commonly referred to as pufferfish that store the toxin in high concentrations in its liver. [9] Due to the heat-stable nature of the toxin, it remains active even after cooking and results in the poisoning of those who eat improperly prepared fish. The toxin gets absorbed and travels systemically, where it can bind to sodium channels in a variety of tissues. The prevention of contractility of both cardiac and respiratory myocytes can lead to respiratory arrest and cardiac arrest, leading to possible death.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrophysiologic Study Indications and Evaluation -- Indications -- Inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes (Channelopathies)
retrieved_docs.content: Long QT syndrome (LQTS): The risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD) in Long QT syndrome depends on age, sex, and QTc interval duration and ranges from 15 to 70%. [23] Therefore, it is important to identify patients at high risk for SCD. A 12 lead EKG is a very useful risk stratification tool in these patients and QTc> 500ms and presence of T-wave alternans is a useful marker of electrical instability.  Studies show that patients with QT syndrome who have had a previous syncope episode may not elicit VT on programmed electrical stimulation of EP study. Rapid polymorphic VT may be induced in 40% of the patients with the protocol using 3 extra stimuli with and without isoprenaline administration. Moreover, the presence of NSVT has no prognostic value in identifying high-risk patients for future SCD. Therefore, an EP study is not indicated in long QT syndrome. Per ESC Guidelines 2015, the use of EPS is a class III, LOE C for evaluation of patients with LQTS.

retrieved_docs.title: Antiarrhythmic Medications -- Indications -- Class III: K + Channel Blockers/Openers
retrieved_docs.content: Kv1.5 (ultra-rapid K + current) blockers (eg, vernakalant): pharmacological cardioversion of recent atrial fibrillation (in patients without structural heart disease /ischemic heart disease; not-FDA approved) [12] [13] IIIb: Metabolically dependent K + channel blockers (eg, nicorandil, pinacidil): treatment of stable angina (second line), decreased actional potential recovery time, shortened QT intervals.

retrieved_docs.title: Flecainide -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Flecainide acts on the fast-inward Na+ ion channel and has a high affinity to activated or open Na+ channels. It prolongs depolarization and increases refractoriness due to slow release from its binding site. It potently acts on the His-Purkinje system. It also works by inhibiting IKr channels, delaying potassium rectifier current, resulting in prolongation of action potential duration in both ventricular and atrial muscle fibers. Flecainide is shown to block ryanodine receptor opening, which reduces calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum resulting after depolarization and triggered activity. [1] Hence, indications for flecainide include catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT).

retrieved_docs.title: Jervell and Lange-Nielsen Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiac action potential comprises a depolarization, a plateau/refractory, and rapid repolarization phases. The repolarization phase is seen on the ECG as the QTc interval. It is associated with an increase in potassium permeability, which moves potassium out of the cardiac cell. This repolarizing potassium current, the delayed outward rectifier current, has a rapidly activating component and a slowly activating component. [4] Mutations in the KCNQ1 and KCNE1 genes encode the alpha and beta subunits of the slowly activating components resulting in a slower movement of potassium outside the cell, which prolongs the QTc interval. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Myotonia Congenita -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Potassium channels have emerged as a novel therapeutic target. The potassium channel activator retigabine has been investigated in murine models of myotonia congenita, where it significantly reduced myotonia severity in vivo . However, evidence to guide pharmacological management is limited. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Mexiletine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Lei et al discuss a modern classification of antiarrhythmic drugs, incorporating advances that have been made since the original Vaughan Williams classification was introduced in 1970. [14] It incorporates findings related to Nav1.5 which is preferentially expressed in atrial, Purkinje conducting and ventricular as opposed to SAN and AVN cardiomyocyte. Class Ib drugs bind preferentially to the Nav1.5 inactivated state from which they dissociate relatively rapidly making class Ib drugs particularly effective in reducing ventricular arrhythmias due to Nav 1.5 channels remaining inactive for the longest duration in ventricular tissue. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Atrial Fibrillation -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most commonly, hypertension, structural, valvular, and ischemic heart disease illicit the paroxysmal and persistent forms of atrial fibrillation, but the underlying pathophysiology is not well understood. Some research has shown evidence of genetic causes of atrial fibrillation involving chromosome 10 (10q22-q24) that consists of a mutation in the gene alpha-subunit of the cardiac Ik5, which is responsible for pore formation. This is a gain of function mutation, allowing for more pores, increasing the activity within the ion channels of the heart, and thus affecting the stability of the membrane and reducing its refractory time. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of ventricular tachycardia target the intrinsic accelerated ventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل) and its subtype ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني). Understanding these interconnected entities—electrical conduction abnormalities, structural cardiac changes, specific arrhythmic syndromes, diagnostic measurements, and therapeutic interventions—is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management of patients with cardiac rhythm disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2024
retrieved_docs.content: The electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation; the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia; and the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of ventricular tachycardia are all classified as دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية, representing cardiac electrophysiology studies that deliberately induce arrhythmias, identify arrhythmogenic foci, and apply catheter ablation to treat the respective rhythm disorders. In the atrial fibrillation procedure, the primary focus is الرجفان الأذيني, whereas the ventricular tachycardia procedure targets التسارع البطيني, which is a subtype of نظمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل (self‑accelerated ventricular rhythm). Clinically, الرجفان الأذيني is associated with مرض قلبي روماتزمي, indicating a link between atrial fibrillation and rheumatic heart disease. Pharmacologically, both drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate are contraindicated in patients with الرجفان الأذيني, underscoring the need to avoid these agents in individuals with atrial fibrillation. Together, these relationships highlight the diagnostic and therapeutic role of cardiac electrophysiology studies, the specific arrhythmic targets they address, the disease associations of atrial fibrillation, and important medication contraindications that inform safe patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1718
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmic drugs represent a diverse pharmacologic group used to treat cardiac rhythm disturbances. Among them, cardiac glycosides are classified as antiarrhythmics because they modulate myocardial contractility and conduction. Nizofenone, although primarily known as a neuroprotective compound, also exhibits antiarrhythmic activity and is considered part of the antiarrhythmic category. Prokainamide hydrochloride is a well‑established class IA antiarrhythmic agent that blocks sodium channels and prolongs the action potential. Together, these agents illustrate the broad spectrum of mechanisms encompassed by antiarrhythmic therapy, highlighting the importance of selecting appropriate agents based on their specific pharmacologic properties for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: techniques such as cardiac rhythm stimulation, focal ablation (“استئصال بؤرة الاضطراب”), and specific anti‑acceleration therapies, which are essential components of clinical care. Overall, the network underscores the interdependence of electrical conduction abnormalities, tachyarrhythmias, structural heart disease, renal involvement, and therapeutic constraints in comprehensive cardiac patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2557
retrieved_docs.content: The electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation and the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia are both classified as a دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية (cardiac electrophysiological study). Each procedure involves deliberately inducing arrhythmias, identifying the arrhythmogenic focus, performing ablation of that focus, and delivering therapeutic treatment targeted to a specific rhythm disorder—atrial fibrillation in the first case and supraventricular tachycardia in the second. As forms of cardiac electrophysiological study, they provide essential diagnostic insight and therapeutic options for managing abnormal heart rhythms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1526
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmics, class Ib are a group of sodium‑channel–blocking agents that preferentially affect depolarized cardiac tissue, and mexiletin is a member of this class. As a class Ib antiarrhythmic, mexiletin is employed in the management of ventricular arrhythmias and other rhythm disturbances, reflecting the pharmacologic properties shared by drugs within the Antiarrhythmics, class Ib category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: be used to treat تسرع القلب الانتيابي, but it is contraindicated in إِحْصار القلب غير محدد, indicating that while adenosine can terminate certain tachycardias, it should be avoided in patients with unspecified heart block due to the risk of exacerbating conduction failure. Together, these relationships delineate a network in which various rhythm disorders, their anatomical substrates, therapeutic options, and contraindications are interlinked, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing cardiac conduction and rhythm abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1789
retrieved_docs.content: Triamteren is classified as a blokátory sodíkových kanálů (sodium channel blocker). Sodium channel blockers act by inhibiting the rapid influx of sodium ions during the depolarization phase of cardiac myocytes, thereby decreasing excitability and slowing conduction velocity in the heart. This pharmacologic class is employed in the treatment of various cardiac arrhythmias, where modulation of sodium currents helps to restore normal rhythm and improve electrical stability. Consequently, triamteren shares these mechanistic properties and therapeutic implications inherent to sodium channel–blocking agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 218
retrieved_docs.content: studies—including arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and targeted treatment of atrial fibrillation, supraventricular tachycardia, and ventricular tachycardia—address the underlying conduction defects. Pharmacologic therapy comprises agents such as adenosine monophosphate (effective for antidromic tachycardia but contraindicated in unspecified heart block) and acknowledges contraindications of drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate in atrial fibrillation. Device therapy with pacemakers (kardiostimulátor) and transvenous lead placement is employed, particularly in neonates with paroxysmal atrial tachycardia. Overall, disturbances in impulse propagation generate a continuum of rhythm and rate disorders that intersect with structural heart disease, require integrated electro‑diagnostic assessment, and demand individualized pharmacologic, ablative, and device‑based management, especially when rheumatic valvular pathology co‑exists.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2747
retrieved_docs.content: Congestive cardiomyopathy (اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني) is clinically associated with both supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) and atrial flutter (رفرفة أذينية). These arrhythmic conditions frequently arise in the setting of congestive cardiomyopathy, reflecting a close pathophysiological link in which impaired myocardial function predisposes patients to rapid supraventricular rhythms and organized atrial flutter circuits. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as the presence of supraventricular tachycardia or atrial flutter may signal underlying congestive cardiomyopathy and guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at both the structural heart disease and its rhythm complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 689
retrieved_docs.content: with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of ventricular tachycardia”—are all classified as types of “دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية” (cardiac electrophysiology study). The atrial‑fibrillation‑focused study specifically targets atrial fibrillation (الرجفان الأذيني), while the ventricular‑tachycardia‑focused study targets ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني). Ventricular acceleration is a subtype of an accelerated idioventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Atrial fibrillation is clinically associated with rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي) and with transient ischemic attacks, reflecting its role in both structural heart disease and cerebrovascular events. Pharmacologically, the hormonal agents drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate are contraindicated in patients with atrial fibrillation, underscoring the need for careful medication selection in this arrhythmia. Overall, the interconnections among transient cerebral ischemia,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2870
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias are classified under the broader category تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ and frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية. Recognizing that cardiac dysrhythmias belong to the supraventricular tachycardia family and are often associated with atrial flutter helps clinicians anticipate related rhythm disturbances, guide diagnostic evaluation, and tailor therapeutic strategies for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2476
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmics, class Ia is a pharmacologic subclass of antiarrhythmic agents that encompasses the drugs ajmalin and prajmalin. Both ajmalin and prajmalin belong to this class, sharing the characteristic sodium‑channel blocking mechanism and similar clinical applications in the management of ventricular and supraventricular arrhythmias. Recognizing that Antiarrhythmics, class Ia includes both ajmalin and prajmalin clarifies its classification and supports appropriate therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2507
retrieved_docs.content: Dronedaron and ibutilide are classified as members of Antiarrhythmics, class III. As class III antiarrhythmic agents, both drugs primarily block cardiac potassium channels, prolonging the action potential and refractory period to help maintain normal sinus rhythm in various arrhythmias. Recognizing that dronedaron and ibutilide belong to the Antiarrhythmics, class III group informs clinicians about their similar electrophysiological effects, therapeutic indications, and shared safety considerations, such as the potential for torsades de pointes, thereby guiding appropriate selection and monitoring of antiarrhythmic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2136
retrieved_docs.content: Dofetilide is classified as a Class III antiarrhythmic agent and its therapeutic action is mediated through an inverse effect on the I_Kr (rapid delayed‑rectifier potassium) current, thereby prolonging cardiac repolarization and helping to control abnormal heart rhythms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: The electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation, the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia, and the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of ventricular tachycardia are all classified as دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية (cardiac electrophysiological studies). These studies are designed to induce arrhythmias, locate arrhythmogenic foci, and perform ablation to treat the targeted rhythm disorder. The atrial fibrillation study specifically has focus on الرجفان الأذيني (atrial fibrillation), while the ventricular tachycardia study has focus on التسارع البطيني (ventricular acceleration), which itself is a subtype of نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل (intrinsic accelerated ventricular system). In pharmacologic management, the hormonal agents drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate are contraindicated in patients with الرجفان الأذيني, indicating that these drugs should be avoided in individuals with atrial fibrillation. Together, these relationships highlight the role of cardiac electrophysiology studies in diagnosing and treating specific arrhythmias and underscore important medication contraindications for atrial fibrillation, informing both procedural planning and therapeutic decision‑making.
